questions.text: A critical bug is found an hour before release. What do you do?

answers.text: Jump in and fix it yourself — you're fastest
Walk the engineer through debugging it so they learn
Take command of the situation — triage, delegate, and clear the path
Revert the risky change and ship what's stable now

questions.text: How do you know a project is on track?

answers.text: I talk to people — their tone and energy tell me everything
Dashboards, burndown charts, and status reports
Are we shipping increments regularly? Then we're fine
I check that every open question has an owner and a deadline

questions.text: A junior engineer is stuck on a task. How do you help?

answers.text: Pair with them and show them how to do it
Ask questions until they find the answer themselves
Point them to the right documentation and check back later
Reassign it — put the right person on the right problem

questions.text: Someone proposes replacing a core system with new technology. Your reaction?

answers.text: We tried that in 2019. Let me tell you what happened.
Let's prototype it in a week and see if it actually works
Show me the migration plan, rollback strategy, and success metrics
If the team's excited about it, that energy is worth exploring

questions.text: How do you prefer to work on a big problem?

answers.text: Give me the problem, close the door, and I'll come back with an answer
Break it into small pieces and start shipping the first one today
Understand the full scope before writing a single line of code
Figure out who's confused about what and start clarifying

questions.text: What frustrates you most at work?

answers.text: Nobody knows who's responsible for what
People keep reinventing wheels that already work fine
We over-plan and under-ship
We ship sloppy work and call it 'iterating'

questions.text: Your team has a conflict between two senior engineers. What do you do?

answers.text: Talk to each privately, read the real dynamic, then bring them together
Make the call yourself — someone has to decide
Set up a structured decision framework so it's not personal
Let them work it out — they're adults

questions.text: A feature is 90% done but the last 10% is painful. What now?

answers.text: Ship the 90% — users get value now, we polish later
Finish it properly — the last 10% is what separates good from great
I'll just knock out that last 10% myself this weekend
Identify exactly what's blocking and assign clear owners for each piece
Agenda, time-boxed, decisions documented, next actions assigned
I avoid meetings — just send me the doc and I'll comment
Casual and conversational — the best info comes out when people relax
Short and decisive — here's the problem, here's what we're doing, go

questions.text: Your company announces a major reorg. Your first thought?

answers.text: Who's going to be confused? Let me get ahead of the chaos
I've seen this movie before — I know how it ends
Let me talk to my people and find out how they're really feeling
New structure, new opportunities — where's the most interesting problem now?
